title: Name what you feel
description: Put the right word on a feeling and it loosens its grip.
summaryPoints: Name a feeling in plain words to calm your brain's alarm
Swap vague labels like 'bad' for precise ones like 'lonely' or 'overwhelmed'
Read your body's cues — tight chest, clenched jaw — as early signals
Map any feeling by two things: how pleasant it is and how much energy it has
Treat every emotion as information, not as good or bad

content.title: The skill nobody taught you
content.body:
You can ace a test and still get wrecked by a group chat going quiet on you.

Emotional intelligence is noticing, understanding, and managing emotions — yours and other people's. It's not a personality you're stuck with; it's a skill you build, like free throws.

And it all starts with one tiny move: **naming what you feel**.

content.title: Name it to tame it
content.body:
When something hits you hard, a little alarm in your brain — the **amygdala** — goes off. You feel flooded.

Here's the trick: the moment you put the feeling into actual words — "I'm anxious," "I'm hurt" — that alarm quiets down and your thinking brain comes back online.

Naming a feeling doesn't make it bigger. It hands you the steering wheel.

content.prompt: Saying 'I'm really anxious right now' out loud makes the feeling more intense.
content.explanation: It's the opposite: naming a feeling calms your brain's alarm and brings your thinking brain back. Name it to tame it.

content.prompt: Putting a feeling into words calms the brain's alarm center, the ___.
content.explanation: The amygdala fires the alarm; labeling the feeling turns the volume down so you can think clearly.

content.title: Vague feelings, vague choices
content.body:
"I feel bad." "I'm stressed." Those are foggy — and a foggy feeling gives you nothing to work with.

Get **precise** and the fog clears. Are you **overwhelmed**, **disappointed**, **lonely**, or **jealous**? Each one points to a totally different next move.

The more exact words you have for feelings, the better your choices get. That precision even has a name: **emotional granularity**.

content.prompt: What's the most precise way to name this?
content.context: Two friends posted a photo from a hangout you weren't invited to. You catch yourself just saying 'I feel bad.'
content.explanation: 'Left out' names what actually happened. A precise word points you toward a real response, while 'bad' just keeps you stuck.

content.prompt: Trade each vague word for a more precise one.

pairs.left: Bad with way too much going on

content.title: Your body tells you first
content.body:
Your body often clocks a feeling before your mind finds the word.

- Tight chest or shallow breath
- Clenched jaw or fists
- Butterflies or a sinking stomach
- Hot face, racing heart

Think of these as an **early-warning system**. Catch the cue early and you get to choose your next move — instead of reacting after you've already snapped.
content.imageAlt: A body outline with emotion cue markers at the chest, jaw, and stomach

content.prompt: Match each body cue to the feeling it often signals.

content.instructions: One word breaks this sentence. Tap it.
content.sentence: Your body signals a feeling before your mind ignores the right word.
content.explanation: Body cues come first as an early warning, so your mind can find the word — not ignore it.

content.title: The mood meter
content.body:
There's a clean way to map any feeling using just two dials:

- **Pleasantness** — does this feel good or bad?
- **Energy** — high and revved up, or low and quiet?

That gives four zones: **Red** (high energy, unpleasant — angry, anxious), **Yellow** (high energy, pleasant — excited, joyful), **Blue** (low energy, unpleasant — sad, drained), **Green** (low energy, pleasant — calm, content).
content.imageAlt: A 2x2 mood grid with red, yellow, blue, and green quadrants

content.context: It's the morning of a trip you've been counting down to. You're bouncing, can't sit still, grinning at your phone.
content.explanation: High energy plus pleasant lands you in Yellow — excited, joyful, buzzing. Same high energy turned unpleasant would be Red.

content.prompt: Feelings in the Red and Blue zones are 'bad' emotions you should shut down.
content.explanation: No feeling is bad — they're all information. Anger and sadness are telling you something matters; the goal is to read them, not silence them.

content.prompt: Last one: drop each feeling into its zone on the mood meter.

items.text: Furious you got blamed for a group project mess that wasn't yours
Buzzing with excitement the night before a concert
Drained and flat after a brutal week of deadlines
Calm and content after finally talking it out with a friend
Anxious refreshing your phone, waiting for a reply
Cozy and relaxed on the couch with nowhere to be

content.explanation: The RULER mood meter maps any feeling by pleasantness and energy. No feeling is 'bad' — each one is information about what you need next.
